ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for otah bun; mapped to closest parent bread product class (FOODON_03315595). Otah bun is a filled baked bread product containing spiced fish paste (otah/otak-otak).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for otah bun. Mapped to A000E (Bread and similar products) as the closest defensible parent category for a filled baked bread product containing spiced fish paste.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for otah bun or a closely equivalent filled bread product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard baked otah bun weighs approximately 100 g and constitutes a typical single-serve snack or light meal portion as commonly sold at Singaporean bakeries and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Baked Otah Bun — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Baked Otah Bun (cr_id F05020003). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and sugar are estimated from standard otah bun composition using HPB and USDA SR Legacy data for the bread and fish paste components, as these specific sub-fields are not individually reported in the HPB summary record.

gi_source.title: White bread GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to baked otah bun
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baked otah bun. The bun component is made from refined white wheat flour, which has a GI of approximately 70–75 per the University of Sydney GI Database (white bread reference, GI ~70). The otah fish paste filling is low-carbohydrate and does not materially alter the overall GI. A GI of 70 is applied as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (70 × 42.0) / 100 = 29.4 per 100 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 394 mg/100 g with insufficient potassium (≈150 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (2 g/100 g) to offset the sodium burden
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit baked otah bun to one bun per occasion, avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods, and request reduced-salt preparation where possible, as the sodium content of 394 mg/100 g approaches the upper borderline threshold and the low potassium-to-sodium ratio does not provide meaningful cardiovascular offset.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (42 g/100 g) from white wheat flour with low dietary fibre (2 g/100 g), yielding a high glycemic index (~70) and glycemic load (~29 per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit intake to half a bun per serving, pair with a protein- or fibre-rich food to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and choose wholemeal bun variants when available, as the high GI and low fibre content of the refined white flour bun will cause a significant post-meal blood glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (394 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~3.5 g/100 g from coconut milk in the otah filling) with low dietary fibre (2 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, baked otah bun should be consumed in moderation (no more than one bun per occasion) due to its borderline-high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from coconut milk; the anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, galangal) in the otah filling provide a partial offsetting benefit but do not outweigh the sodium and saturated fat concerns.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger, galangal) in otah filling are beneficial, but high sodium (394 mg/100 g) and nightshade ingredients (chilli) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Baked otah bun may be consumed by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis in moderation; the curcumin in turmeric and gingerols in ginger/galangal provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit, but those with nightshade sensitivity should monitor for symptom aggravation from chilli, and high sodium should be managed by requesting reduced-salt preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked otah bun (also spelled otak-otak bun) is a popular Singaporean baked snack consisting of a soft white wheat flour bun encasing a filling of otah — a spiced, aromatic fish paste made from blended fish (commonly Spanish mackerel or tenggiri), coconut milk, turmeric, galangal, lemongrass, chilli, ginger, shallots, and egg. The bun is baked until golden, yielding a soft, slightly chewy exterior with a moist, fragrant, mildly spicy fish paste interior. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly spicy, and aromatic from the Southeast Asian spice blend. Otah buns are widely sold at Singaporean bakeries, hawker centres, and convenience stores, and are commonly eaten as breakfast, a morning snack, or a light meal. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 284 kcal, 10.6 g protein, 8.2 g fat, and 42 g carbohydrate, with 2 g dietary fibre and 394 mg sodium. The refined white flour bun gives the product a high glycemic index (~70), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The otah filling contributes anti-inflammatory polyphenols via turmeric (curcumin) and ginger/galangal, which are used in material quantities in traditional otah paste. Allergens include gluten (wheat), fish, and egg. The product is generally considered halal-suitable in Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a baked otah bun?
Is otah bun suitable for diabetics?
Is otah bun high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Baked Otah Bun (cr_id F05020003) with a recorded confidence of 0.95. Saturated fat and sugar are estimated from component-level data (HPB and USDA SR Legacy) and are clearly documented as estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database white bread reference, which is the closest defensible approved-source equivalent.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100 g) is estimated from the coconut milk content of the otah filling and the wheat flour bun base using HPB and USDA SR Legacy component data; not directly reported in the HPB summary record. 2. Sugar (5.0 g/100 g) is estimated from standard white bread and otah paste component data; not directly reported in the HPB summary record. 3. Potassium (150 mg/100 g) is estimated from component-level data; not directly reported in the HPB summary record. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g based on the absence of hydrogenated fats in traditional otah bun preparation. 5. The following fields are set to -1 because detailed data for these nutrients is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this specific composite dish (cr_id F05020003) as of 2026-04-16, and no sufficiently close equivalent entry providing these values was found in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or ICMR-NIN: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g — detailed fatty acid fractions not reported in HPB Foodo for this composite dish; cholesterol_mg — not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg — full micronutrient panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this composite dish and no sufficiently close USDA SR Legacy composite equivalent was identified; phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg — mineral panel not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry; water_g — moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this entry. All -1 values reflect genuine data unavailability from approved sources and are not estimated. 6. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for baked otah bun was found in any approved GI source. 7. Halal status is assessed as likely true based on standard Singaporean commercial otah bun ingredients; individual products should be verified against MUIS certification. 8. The input record listed the condition key as 'diabetes'; this has been updated to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas scoring rules. 9. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 entries, as no exact term exists for otah bun in either ontology.
